narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows determination to pursue a legal career, but the admissions model focused on key predictors of bar exam success has flagged some concerns. The model predicts a 70.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This decision hinged most on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed you in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. That low Year 3 decile was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead, even compared to your similarly low Year 1 decile, which also weighed against you but had slightly less influence. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction toward passing, performing close to the average of 3.21 and showing you can handle college-level work. These three elements—your Year 3 decile as the top influence, followed by Year 1 decile, and then GPA—dominated the model's thinking, overshadowing other details like your lower-middle family income or LSAT score of 29, which is below the usual 36.7 average. For context, most admitted students rank higher in school deciles and boast stronger test scores, placing you below typical benchmarks in the areas that mattered most here. It's tough to hear, especially when you're eyeing full-time study like 93% of peers, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model doing its job based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking academic advising to improve your standing in advanced coursework, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment. Building a stronger transcript now could flip the script for your next application, proving the model wrong through real progress. You've got the GPA foundation; channeling that into targeted improvements will open doors. Keep pushing—many successful lawyers faced early setbacks like this.
